title: Ask what exponent: logarithms and exponential change

objectives.statement: Translate between logarithmic and exponential statements while enforcing positive-argument and valid-base constraints.
Use product, quotient, and power laws to transform logarithmic expressions without inventing a false sum rule.
Solve elementary exponential equations with logarithms and verify solutions in the original equation.
Interpret growth factors, decay factors, doubling times, and half-lives while distinguishing discrete and continuous parameterizations.

scenes.title: The unknown is in the exponent
scenes.contentHtml:
The unknown is in the exponent

A fictional archive begins with 250 indexed objects and doubles after each completed cycle. When will it first exceed 10,000 objects?

The model is N=250(2t)N=250(2^t). Dividing by 250 gives 2t=402^t=40. The question is no longer ordinary multiplication: what exponent on 2 produces 40?

That exponent is t=log2⁡40≈5.32t=\log_2 40\approx5.32. If only completed cycles count, the first exceeding cycle is 6 because 250(25)=8000250(2^5)=8000 and 250(26)=16000250(2^6)=16000.

This lesson uses logarithms to recover unknown exponents, then checks the answer in the original change model.

Starting check

  • Rewrite 34=813^4=81 as a logarithmic statement.
  • Can a real logarithm have argument zero?
  • Does log⁡(4+6)\log(4+6) equal log⁡4+log⁡6\log4+\log6?

If powers are uncertain, return briefly to exponent laws and write a small table of 2t2^t before continuing.

Source Note
claims claim-log-inverse, claim-log-ratiosources source-nist-dlmf-log, source-nist-si-log

NIST sources document logarithm functions and logarithms of dimensionless ratios. The archive, threshold, and teaching sequence are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

All symbolic relationships are restated in words. No task depends on a plotted curve, color, timing, or calculator layout. Read logb⁡x\log_b x aloud as “log base b of x.”

scenes.title: A logarithm names an exponent
scenes.contentHtml:
A logarithm names an exponent

For b>0b>0, b≠1b\ne1, and x>0x>0,

logb⁡x=yif and only ifby=x.\log_b x=y\quad\text{if and only if}\quad b^y=x.

The base restrictions make the exponential function one-to-one over the reals; the positive argument restriction reflects that byb^y is positive.

Definition
id definition-common-natural-log

log⁡x\log x commonly denotes base 10 in this lesson, while ln⁡x\ln x denotes base ee. Both answer an exponent question; the chosen base sets the exponential scale.

Examples: log10⁡1000=3\log_{10}1000=3, log2⁡(1/8)=−3\log_2(1/8)=-3, and ln⁡e4=4\ln e^4=4.

Laws from exponent structure

For positive M,NM,N,

logb⁡(MN)=logb⁡M+logb⁡N,\log_b(MN)=\log_bM+\log_bN,
logb⁡(M/N)=logb⁡M−logb⁡N,\log_b(M/N)=\log_bM-\log_bN,
logb⁡(Mp)=plogb⁡M.\log_b(M^p)=p\log_bM.

These are exponent laws in inverse form. Change of base follows by solving by=xb^y=x with natural logs:

logb⁡x=ln⁡xln⁡b.\log_bx=\frac{\ln x}{\ln b}.

Exponential change

A discrete model Q(t)=Q0atQ(t)=Q_0a^t has growth when a>1a>1 and decay when the factor aa lies strictly between zero and one. A continuous form Q(t)=Q0ektQ(t)=Q_0e^{kt} has growth for k>0k>0 and decay for k<0k<0. They are equivalent when a=eka=e^k for the same time unit.

Logarithms require a positive dimensionless argument. In applied equations, form a ratio such as Q/Q0Q/Q_0 before taking a log.

Source Note
claims claim-log-inverse, claim-log-laws, claim-log-ratiosources source-nist-dlmf-log, source-nist-si-log

NIST DLMF supplies authoritative logarithm definitions; NIST SI guidance uses logarithms of like-quantity ratios. The elementary derivations and discrete/continuous comparison are original.

scenes.title: Solve a threshold time and check it
scenes.contentHtml:
Solve a threshold time and check it

A fictional counter follows N(t)=320(1.18)tN(t)=320(1.18)^t. Find when it reaches 1000 under the model.

Worked Example
id worked-example-threshold

1. Isolate the exponential factor.

1000320=1.18t,\frac{1000}{320}=1.18^t,

so 3.125=1.18t3.125=1.18^t.

2. Estimate. Since 1.185≈2.291.18^5\approx2.29 and 1.1810≈5.231.18^10\approx5.23, the time lies between 5 and 10 intervals.

3. Take logs of both positive sides.

ln⁡3.125=tln⁡1.18,\ln3.125=t\ln1.18,
t=ln⁡3.125ln⁡1.18≈6.88.t=\frac{\ln3.125}{\ln1.18}\approx6.88.

4. Check. 320(1.18)6.88≈1000320(1.18)^{6.88}\approx1000; the result lies inside the estimated interval.

5. Interpret. The continuous-time threshold under this fictional model is about 6.88 intervals. If observations occur only after completed intervals, compare interval 6 and 7 rather than rounding without context.

Check
id check-log-solution

Why must the ratio 1000/3201000/320 be formed before taking a logarithm?

It isolates the dimensionless growth factor and keeps the logarithm's argument positive and comparable to 1.18t1.18^t.

Source Note
claims claim-log-inverse, claim-log-laws, claim-log-ratiosources source-nist-dlmf-log, source-nist-si-log

The counter, numbers, derivation, and interpretation are original applications of the sourced logarithm framework.

scenes.title: Logs split products, not sums
scenes.contentHtml:
Logs split products, not sums

Misconception
id misconception-log-sum

The valid product law does not imply log⁡(M+N)=log⁡M+log⁡N\log(M+N)=\log M+\log N.

For base 10, log⁡(10+90)=log⁡100=2\log(10+90)=\log100=2, while log⁡10+log⁡90=1+log⁡90≈2.95\log10+\log90=1+\log90\approx2.95.

The law works for products because bubv=bu+vb^u b^v=b^{u+v}. Addition inside a logarithm has no corresponding exponent law.

Domain checking also comes first. An algebraic transformation that asks for ln⁡(−3)\ln(-3) has left the real-logarithm domain even if later calculator steps produce an error code rather than an explanation.

Source Note
claims claim-log-inverse, claim-log-lawssources source-nist-dlmf-log

The counterexample and misconception repair are original mathematical synthesis from the logarithm definition.

scenes.title: Move between exponent and logarithm
scenes.contentHtml:
Move between exponent and logarithm

1. Translate

Rewrite 5−2=1/255^{-2}=1/25 with a logarithm.

Feedback: log5⁡(1/25)=−2\log_5(1/25)=-2.

2. Expand validly

Expand ln⁡(3x2/y)\ln(3x^2/y) for x≠0x\ne0 and y>0y>0.

Feedback: ln⁡3+2ln⁡|x|−ln⁡y\ln3+2\ln|x|-\ln y. The absolute value keeps the squared factor compatible with the real logarithm when x<0x<0.

3. Solve

Solve 4t=704^t=70.

Feedback: t=ln⁡70/ln⁡4≈3.065t=\ln70/\ln4\approx3.065. Check that 43=644^3=64 places the answer just above 3.

4. Read a decay factor

In Q=Q0(0.82)tQ=Q_0(0.82)^t, state the percentage change per interval.

Feedback: The factor 0.82 retains 82%, so the quantity decreases by 18% per interval.

Recovery route

  • Logarithmic and exponential forms do not match: identify base, exponent, and result in that order.
  • A log law is applied to a sum: recombine or evaluate the sum before logging.
  • Negative or zero argument: inspect the real domain before calculating.
  • Time has the wrong sign: check whether the factor represents growth or decay.
  • Decimal answer seems opaque: bracket it between nearby integer powers and substitute back.
Source Note
claims claim-log-inverse, claim-log-laws, claim-log-ratiosources source-nist-dlmf-log, source-nist-si-log

All practice, feedback, and recovery guidance are original.

scenes.title: Recover a half-life from two observations
scenes.contentHtml:
Recover a half-life from two observations

A fictional signal follows A(t)=A0(1/2)t/hA(t)=A_0(1/2)^{t/h}, where h>0h>0 is its half-life. After 14 hours, the ratio A/A0A/A_0 is 0.20. Estimate hh.

Feedback after a complete attempt

0.20=2−14/h.0.20=2^{-14/h}.

Take logs of the positive dimensionless ratio:

ln⁡0.20=−14hln⁡2.\ln0.20=-\frac{14}{h}\ln2.

Therefore

h=−14ln⁡2ln⁡0.20≈6.03 h.h=-\frac{14\ln2}{\ln0.20}\approx6.03\text{ h}.

The sign is sensible: both ln⁡0.20\ln0.20 and the exponent are negative, while hh is positive. A reverse check gives (1/2)14/6.03≈0.20(1/2)^{14/6.03}\approx0.20.

This estimates a parameter inside the stated fictional model; it does not establish that any measured system follows exact exponential decay.

Source Note
claims claim-log-inverse, claim-log-laws, claim-log-ratiosources source-nist-dlmf-log, source-nist-si-log

The signal model, values, solution, and boundary statement are original.

scenes.title: Rebuild the inverse-change method
scenes.contentHtml:
Rebuild the inverse-change method

  1. State the exponential model and time unit.
  2. Isolate a positive dimensionless ratio.
  3. Translate “unknown exponent” into a logarithm.
  4. Apply log laws only to products, quotients, and powers.
  5. Solve, bracket the scale, and substitute into the original model.

Retrieval

  • What does logb⁡x=y\log_bx=y say exponentially?
  • Why must a real logarithm's argument be positive?
  • Which exponent law produces the logarithm product law?
  • How are a discrete factor aa and continuous rate kk related?

Complete assessment.json. If translation fails, return to the definition. If algebra works but the domain fails, revisit the misconception. If a time is uninterpreted, redo the threshold example and distinguish continuous time from completed intervals.

Source Note
claims claim-log-inverse, claim-log-laws, claim-log-ratiosources source-nist-dlmf-log, source-nist-si-log

The synthesis uses authoritative logarithm definitions and dimensionless-ratio guidance through an original inverse-change procedure.

assessment.title: Logarithms and exponential change mastery gate

items.prompt: Which statement is equivalent to log base 3 of (1/27) = -3?

answer.summary: The base raised to the logarithm value equals the argument.
answer.reasoning: The inverse definition gives 3 to the power -3 equals 1/27.
commonErrors: Using the logarithm value as the base

items.prompt: Expand ln(5x³/y²) for x > 0 and y > 0.

answer.summary: Use product, quotient, and power laws.
answer.reasoning: The product becomes a sum, the quotient a difference, and exponents become coefficients.
commonErrors: Splitting a sum that is not present

items.prompt: Solve 3e^(0.4t) = 21. Give t to three decimal places and check by substitution.

answer.correct: e^(0.4t) = 7, so 0.4t = ln 7 and t = ln 7/0.4 ≈ 4.865. Substitution gives 3e^(0.4×4.865) ≈ 21.
answer.reasoning: The exponential factor is isolated before applying the natural logarithm to both positive sides.
acceptableResponses: 4.864 or 4.865 with valid working and check
commonErrors: Taking ln before dividing by 3 and then splitting a sum

items.prompt: Over the reals, what is the domain of f(x) = ln(x - 4)?

answer.summary: The logarithm argument must be strictly positive.
answer.reasoning: x - 4 > 0 implies x > 4; zero is not an allowed real logarithm argument.
commonErrors: Excluding only the single zero point while allowing negative arguments

items.prompt: A fictional exponential signal is 12.5% of its initial value after 18 hours. Under an exact half-life model, find the half-life and explain without decimal logarithms if possible.

answer.correct: 12.5% = 0.125 = 1/8 = (1/2)³, so 18 hours contains three half-lives and h = 18/3 = 6 hours.
answer.reasoning: Recognizing the exact power of one half makes the unknown exponent visible without approximation.
acceptableResponses: A logarithmic derivation that obtains 6 h
commonErrors: Treating a 12.5% remaining fraction as a 12.5% decrease

items.prompt: A fictional process follows P(t) = 450e^(-0.12t), with t in hours. Find when P reaches 90, determine the model's half-life, state the domain conditions used in taking logarithms, and verify both results numerically.

answer.correct: For P = 90, the positive dimensionless ratio is 90/450 = 0.2. Thus e^(-0.12t) = 0.2 and t = -ln(0.2)/0.12 ≈ 13.412 h. For half-life, e^(-0.12h) = 0.5, so h = -ln(0.5)/0.12 = ln 2/0.12 ≈ 5.776 h. Checks: 450e^(-0.12×13.412) ≈ 90 and e^(-0.12×5.776) ≈ 0.5. Both log arguments are positive ratios.
answer.reasoning: Each target is divided by the positive initial value, then the natural logarithm inverts the exponential after its positive dimensionless ratio is isolated.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent values within 0.01 h with the same domain statements and checks
commonErrors: Using a positive exponent despite decay
Taking ln of dimensional P without forming a ratio
Confusing the time to 20% with the half-life

criteria.description: Maps base, exponent, and argument correctly.
Uses product, quotient, and power laws to obtain the complete expansion.
Isolates the exponential and applies ln correctly.
Recognizes 0.125 as three factors of one half.
Forms positive dimensionless ratios for both targets.
Verifies both results and states positivity/domain conditions.

claims.statement: For a positive base other than one, the real logarithm is the inverse of exponentiation on positive arguments: log base b of x equals y exactly when b to the y equals x.
claims.scope: Single-valued real logarithms with b > 0, b ≠ 1, and x > 0.
claims.uncertainty: Complex logarithms and branch choices are outside scope.

claims.statement: The logarithm product, quotient, and power laws follow from the corresponding exponent laws when every logarithm argument lies in the real positive domain.
claims.scope: Real logarithms of positive products, quotients, and powers used in this lesson.
claims.uncertainty: There is no analogous general rule that splits a logarithm across addition.

claims.statement: In quantitative applications, a logarithm is taken of a dimensionless number such as a ratio of like quantities rather than a bare dimensional quantity.
claims.scope: The growth, decay, and threshold ratios constructed in the lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Named logarithmic units and discipline-specific reference levels are outside scope.

terms.definition: The exponent to which a valid base must be raised to produce a positive argument.
The logarithm with base e, written ln.
The base-10 logarithm, written log in this lesson when the base is otherwise clear.
A per-interval multiplier greater than one in a discrete exponential model.
A positive per-interval multiplier below one in a discrete exponential model.
The positive time required by an exponential decay model for a quantity to become one half of its reference value.
A quotient of compatible like quantities whose units cancel, leaving a pure number suitable as a logarithm argument.

Originality, evidence, and rights

The “ask what exponent” sequence, archive threshold, counter example, false-sum repair, signal half-life transfer, all values, feedback, recovery, and assessments are original. Every applied setting is fictional. NIST DLMF and SI guidance provide only factual definitions and dimensionless-ratio conventions. No source expression, example, figure, or table is copied or adapted.

NIST rights and public robots routes were checked before access on 2026-07-22. No agent restriction, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party assets, datasets, personal data, or patient material are included.

This mathematical lesson provides no clinical advice and makes no claim of review, approval, publication, measured effectiveness, credit, or competence.
